parts.content:
After the original survey of the $40$ students (with the value of $k$ found in part 1), it was discovered that one student who was recorded as having read $4$ books had actually read $0$ books.

(i) Calculate the new mean number of books for the $40$ students after this correction.

(ii) Without further calculation, state and explain whether the standard deviation will increase or decrease due to this correction.
parts.markscheme:
(i)
<marks> M1 </marks> For calculating the new sum of books: Original $\sum fx = 90$. Change $4$ books to $0$ books, so sum decreases by $4$. New $\sum fx = 90 - 4 = 86$.
<marks> A1 </marks> New mean $= \frac{86}{40} = 2.15$.

(ii)
<marks> A1 </marks> State: The standard deviation will increase.
<marks> R1 </marks> Explanation: The value that changed ($4$) was closer to the mean ($2.25$) than the new value ($0$) is to the new mean ($2.15$). Moving a value further from the mean increases the spread/dispersion.

steps.title: Adjust the total sum
steps.content:
To find the new mean, we first need the new total sum of books read. We start with the original sum (which was $90$) and apply the **Total Sum Adjustment** method. 

We subtract the incorrect value ($4$) and add the correct value ($0$) to the original total:

$$\text{New Sum} = \text{Original Sum} - \text{Incorrect Value} + \text{Correct Value}$$

$$\text{New Sum} = 90 - 4 + 0 = 86$$

gdc.instructions: Type 90 - 4 + 0 and press EXE

ti84.instructions: Type 90 - 4 + 0 and press ENTER

highlights.text: one student who was recorded as having read 4 books had actually read 0 books

steps.title: Calculate the new mean
steps.content:
Now we divide the new sum by the total number of students ($40$) to find the new mean.

$$\text{New Mean} = \frac{\text{New Sum}}{n}$$

$$\text{New Mean} = \frac{86}{40} = 2.15$$

gdc.instructions: Type 86 / 40 and press EXE

ti84.instructions: Type 86 / 40 and press ENTER

steps.title: State effect on standard deviation
steps.content:
For part (ii), we need to determine how the spread of the data changes.

The standard deviation will **increase**.

steps.title: Explain the change
steps.content:
To explain why, we compare how far the changed values are from the mean. The standard deviation measures the average distance of data points from the mean.

*   The value we removed ($4$) was relatively close to the original mean ($2.25$).
*   The value we added ($0$) is further away from the new mean ($2.15$).

Since we replaced a value close to the mean with a value that is more extreme (further away), the overall spread of the data increases.

steps.title: Identify the frequency updates
steps.content:
First, we need to apply the correction to the frequency table. We are told that one student recorded as reading $4$ books actually read $0$ books.

This means we must update the frequencies:
- Decrease the frequency for $x=4$ by $1$: $$5 - 1 = 4$$
- Increase the frequency for $x=0$ by $1$: $$4 + 1 = 5$$

Recall from Part 1 that $k=9$.

highlights.text: one student who was recorded as having read 4 books had actually read 0 books

| **New Freq ($f_{new}$)** | **5** | 10 | 9 | 9 | **4** | 3 |

steps.title: Calculate the new mean
steps.content:
Using the 1-Variable Statistics function on your calculator, read the value for the mean ($\bar{x}$).

$$\text{New Mean } (\bar{x}) = 2.15$$

steps.title: Analyze standard deviation change
steps.content:
For part (ii), we must state if the standard deviation increases or decreases. 

Standard deviation measures the "spread" of data from the mean. We replaced a value of $4$ with a value of $0$. 

- The original mean was $2.25$.
- The new mean is $2.15$.

The removed value ($4$) is closer to the center of the data than the new value ($0$). Moving a data point further away from the mean increases the spread.

steps.title: State final conclusion
steps.content:
Therefore, the standard deviation will **increase**.

**Reason:** The new value ($0$) is further from the new mean ($2.15$) than the old value ($4$) was from the old mean ($2.25$). Adding an extreme value increases the dispersion.

steps.title: Establish the original mean
steps.content:
First, we determine the original mean using the total frequency of 40 students provided in the question. 

Since the sum of frequencies is 40:
$$4 + 10 + k + 9 + 5 + 3 = 40 \Rightarrow k = 9$$

Now, calculate the original sum of books:
$$\text{Original Sum} = (0 \times 4) + (1 \times 10) + (2 \times 9) + (3 \times 9) + (4 \times 5) + (5 \times 3)$$
$$\text{Original Sum} = 0 + 10 + 18 + 27 + 20 + 15 = 90$$

The original mean is:
$$\bar{x}_{old} = \frac{90}{40} = 2.25$$

gdc.instructions: Enter calculation

ti84.instructions: Enter calculation

steps.title: Calculate adjustment per student
steps.content:
One student's record changed from 4 books to 0 books. We can find the new mean by calculating the **mean adjustment** (the change distributed across all students) and adding it to the original mean.

First, find the total change in the sum:
$$\text{Change} = \text{New Value} - \text{Old Value} = 0 - 4 = -4$$

Next, divide this change by the total number of students to find the adjustment per student:
$$\text{Adjustment} = \frac{\text{Change}}{\text{Total Frequency}} = \frac{-4}{40} = -0.1$$

gdc.instructions: Enter -4 divided by 40

ti84.instructions: Enter -4 divided by 40

highlights.text: recorded as having read 4 books had actually read 0 books

steps.title: Apply adjustment to mean
steps.content:
Apply the adjustment directly to the original mean to find the new mean:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{New Mean} &= \text{Original Mean} + \text{Adjustment} \\ &= 2.25 + (-0.1) \\ &= 2.15 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Add adjustment to original mean

ti84.instructions: Add adjustment to original mean

steps.title: State effect on standard deviation
steps.content:
For part (ii), we must state whether the standard deviation increases or decreases.

**Answer:** The standard deviation will **increase**.

steps.title: Explain the change
steps.content:
Standard deviation measures the average spread of data from the mean. To explain the increase, we compare how far the changed value is from the mean before and after the correction.

- The original value ($4$) was distance $|4 - 2.25| = 1.75$ from the mean.
- The new value ($0$) is distance $|0 - 2.15| = 2.15$ from the new mean.

Since the new value ($0$) is further from the center than the original value ($4$) was, the overall spread of the data has increased.

steps.title: Define class intervals
steps.content:
First, we determine the class intervals. The question asks for 5 equal intervals for scores ranging from 1 to 50.

$$ \text{Width} = \frac{\text{Range}}{\text{Number of Intervals}} = \frac{50}{5} = 10 $$

Our five intervals are:
1. $1-10$
2. $11-20$
3. $21-30$
4. $31-40$
5. $41-50$

*   **$1-10$**: $4, 7, 5$ $\rightarrow$ **Frequency: 3**
*   **$11-20$**: $11, 18$ $\rightarrow$ **Frequency: 2**
*   **$21-30$**: $21, 26, 27$ $\rightarrow$ **Frequency: 3**
*   **$31-40$**: $34, 37, 31, 40$ $\rightarrow$ **Frequency: 4**
*   **$41-50$**: $45, 43, 50, 45, 42, 44$ $\rightarrow$ **Frequency: 6**

steps.title: Set window boundaries
steps.content:
This is the most critical step. The question asks for 5 equal class intervals for scores from 1 to 50. This implies an interval width of 10.

To ensure integer values like 10, 20, etc., fall into the correct bins (e.g., 1-10 vs 11-20), we shift the boundaries by 0.5. Standard calculators use left-inclusive bins $[a, b)$, so a boundary at 10 would place the number 10 into the $10-20$ bin, which is incorrect for the interval $1-10$.

Use these settings:
$$\begin{aligned} X_{min} &= 0.5 \\ X_{max} &= 50.5 \\ X_{scl} &= 10 \end{aligned}$$

parts.content: Calculate the mean, standard deviation, and interquartile range (IQR) for this dataset.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the sum of the data: $\sum x = 10 + 12 + 15 + 18 + 20 = 75$

- Calculate the mean: $\bar{x} = \frac{75}{5} = 15$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate the sum of squares or use GDC: $\sum x^2 = 10^2 + 12^2 + 15^2 + 18^2 + 20^2 = 1193$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate the standard deviation: $\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1193}{5} - 15^2} = \sqrt{238.6 - 225} = \sqrt{13.6} \approx 3.69$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Identify the quartiles: $Q_1 = \frac{10+12}{2} = 11$ and $Q_3 = \frac{18+20}{2} = 19$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate the interquartile range: $\text{IQR} = 19 - 11 = 8$ <marks>A1</marks>

5 marks total

steps.content:
First, we calculate the mean ($\bar{x}$) by summing all the data values and dividing by the number of data points ($n=5$).

$$\bar{x} = \frac{\sum x}{n} = \frac{10 + 12 + 15 + 18 + 20}{5}$$

The mean daily profit is $15$ thousand dollars.

steps.title: Sum of squares for deviation
steps.content:
To find the standard deviation manually, it is efficient to use the formula involving the sum of squares: $\sum x^2$.

$$\begin{aligned} \sum x^2 &= 10^2 + 12^2 + 15^2 + 18^2 + 20^2 \\ &= 100 + 144 + 225 + 324 + 400 \\ &= 1193 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate standard deviation
steps.content:
Now we use the standard deviation formula $\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{\sum x^2}{n} - \bar{x}^2}$.

Substituting our values for the sum of squares ($1193$), the number of values ($5$), and the mean ($15$):

$$\begin{aligned} \sigma &= \sqrt{\frac{1193}{5} - 15^2} \\ &= \sqrt{238.6 - 225} \\ &= \sqrt{13.6} \\ &\approx 3.69 \end{aligned}$$

parts.content:
Due to a currency fluctuation, all profit values are increased by $3$ thousand dollars.

Without recalculating, state how the mean, standard deviation, and IQR will change. Explain your reasoning for each.
parts.markscheme:
- State the change in mean with reason: The mean increases by $3$ (to $18$). Adding a constant to all values shifts the distribution by that constant amount. <marks>R1</marks>

- State the change in standard deviation with reason: The standard deviation stays the same. Adding a constant shifts the data but does not change the spread/dispersion. <marks>R1</marks>

- State the change in IQR with reason: The IQR stays the same. The quartiles shift by the same amount, so the difference between them remains constant. <marks>R1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify the transformation
steps.content:
The question asks for the effect of a specific transformation on the summary statistics without performing full calculations. We are told that every data value in the set is increased by a constant amount.

Transformation: $$x_{new} = x_{old} + 3$$

This is relevant to **Topic 4.3**, which covers the effect of constant changes on mean and dispersion.

highlights.text: all profit values are increased by $3$

steps.title: Change in Mean
steps.content:
The mean is a **measure of central tendency** (location). When you add a constant $k$ to every data point, the entire distribution shifts to the right by $k$. Therefore, the center of the data also shifts by exactly that amount.

Since the old mean was $15$ ($75 / 5$), the new mean will be:
$$15 + 3 = 18$$

**Conclusion:** The mean **increases by 3**.

gdc.instructions: Enter data into spreadsheet column A. Create column B = A + 3. Use One-Variable Statistics.

ti84.instructions: Enter data {10,12,15,18,20} into L1. Calculate 1-Var Stats to find x̄ = 15. Then define L2 = L1 + 3. Calculate 1-Var Stats for L2.

desmos.instructions: Define list L=[10,12,15,18,20]. Calculate mean(L). Calculate mean(L+3).

calculator.description: Although the question asks to answer 'without recalculating', you can verify this property by checking the list statistics.

steps.title: Change in Standard Deviation
steps.content:
The standard deviation is a **measure of spread** (dispersion). It measures how far the data points are from the mean.

When we add a constant to every value:
1. The points shift up by $3$.
2. The mean also shifts up by $3$.
3. The *distance* between any point and the mean ($x - μ$) remains exactly the same.

Since the spread of the data relative to the mean has not changed, the standard deviation remains constant.

**Conclusion:** The standard deviation **stays the same**.

steps.title: Change in IQR
steps.content:
The Interquartile Range (IQR) is also a **measure of spread**, calculated as $$IQR = Q_3 - Q_1$$. 

Adding a constant shifts the position of the quartiles:
- The new upper quartile is $Q_3 + 3$
- The new lower quartile is $Q_1 + 3$

The difference between them is:
$$(Q_3 + 3) - (Q_1 + 3) = Q_3 - Q_1$$

The relative distance between the quartiles is preserved.

**Conclusion:** The IQR **stays the same**.

steps.title: Define the transformation
steps.content:
Let the original data values be $x_i$ and the new data values be $y_i$. Since every profit value is increased by $3$, we can express the new values as:

$$y_i = x_i + 3$$

highlights.text: all profit values are increased by $3$

steps.title: Analyze the change in Mean
steps.content:
Using the formula for the mean $\bar{x} = \frac{\sum x_i}{n}$, we can find the new mean $\bar{y}$:

$$\begin{aligned} \bar{y} &= \frac{\sum (x_i + 3)}{n} \\ &= \frac{\sum x_i + \sum 3}{n} \\ &= \frac{\sum x_i + 3n}{n} \\ &= \frac{\sum x_i}{n} + \frac{3n}{n} \\ &= \bar{x} + 3 \end{aligned}$$

The constant is added directly to the mean. The original mean was $15$, so the new mean is $18$.

gdc.instructions: Enter data in spreadsheet, use statistics summary

ti84.instructions: Enter the data list and calculate 1-Var Stats, or sum and divide manually

steps.title: Analyze the Standard Deviation
steps.content:
The standard deviation depends on the deviation from the mean, $(x_i - \bar{x})$. Let's see what happens to this term with the new data $y_i$ and new mean $\bar{y} = \bar{x} + 3$:

$$\begin{aligned} y_i - \bar{y} &= (x_i + 3) - (\bar{x} + 3) \\ &= x_i + 3 - \bar{x} - 3 \\ &= x_i - \bar{x} \end{aligned}$$

The constant $3$ cancels out. Since the deviations $(x_i - \bar{x})$ remain unchanged, the standard deviation stays the same.

steps.title: Analyze the IQR
steps.content:
The Interquartile Range (IQR) is the difference between the third and first quartiles: $Q_3 - Q_1$.

When we add $3$ to the data, both quartiles shift up by $3$:
$$Q_3' = Q_3 + 3$$
$$Q_1' = Q_1 + 3$$

The new IQR is:
$$Q_3' - Q_1' = (Q_3 + 3) - (Q_1 + 3) = Q_3 - Q_1$$

The constant cancels out, so the IQR stays the same.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Based on the algebraic reasoning:

1. **Mean:** Increases by $3$ (to $18$).
2. **Standard Deviation:** Stays the same.
3. **IQR:** Stays the same.

parts.content:
In a different scenario (ignoring the change in part 2), the company decides to double all original daily profit values.

Without recalculating, state how the mean, standard deviation, and IQR will change. Explain your reasoning for each.
parts.markscheme:
- State the change in mean with reason: The mean doubles (to $30$). Multiplying all values by a constant multiplies the mean by that constant. <marks>R1</marks>

- State the change in standard deviation with reason: The standard deviation doubles (to $\approx 7.38$). Multiplying data by a positive constant scales the spread by that constant. <marks>R1</marks>

- State the change in IQR with reason: The IQR doubles (to $16$). The quartiles are doubled, so the difference between them is also doubled. <marks>R1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify the transformation
steps.content:
The question states that the company decides to "double all original daily profit values." Mathematically, this is a linear transformation of the data where every data point $x$ is multiplied by a constant $k = 2$.

We will apply the properties of linear transformations to determine how this affects the mean, standard deviation, and IQR.

highlights.text: double all original daily profit values

steps.title: Effect on the Mean
steps.content:
When a dataset is multiplied by a constant $k$, the mean is also multiplied by that same constant $k$.

Since $k=2$, the new mean will be **double** the original mean.

$$\text{New Mean} = 2 \times \text{Original Mean} = 2(15) = 30$$

steps.title: Effect on Standard Deviation
steps.content:
Standard deviation measures the spread of the data. When data is multiplied by a positive constant $k$, the spread is scaled by that constant.

Therefore, the new standard deviation is **double** the original standard deviation.

$$\text{New SD} = 2 \times \text{Original SD} \approx 2(3.69) \approx 7.38$$

steps.title: Effect on IQR
steps.content:
The Interquartile Range (IQR) is also a measure of spread calculated as $Q_3 - Q_1$. Just like standard deviation, when data is multiplied by a positive constant $k$, the quartiles and the difference between them are scaled by $k$.

Therefore, the new IQR is **double** the original IQR.

$$\text{New IQR} = 2 \times \text{Original IQR} = 2(8) = 16$$

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
Summary of changes:
1.  **Mean**: Doubles. (Multiplication by a constant affects measures of central tendency).
2.  **Standard Deviation**: Doubles. (Multiplication by a constant scales measures of spread).
3.  **IQR**: Doubles. (Multiplication by a constant scales measures of spread).

steps.title: Define the transformation
steps.content:
Let the original data values be $x$ and the new data values be $y$. The problem states that the company decides to double all original daily profit values.

Therefore, for every data point:
$$y = 2x$$

highlights.text: double all original daily profit values

steps.title: Deduce the new mean
steps.content:
Using the definition of the mean $\bar{x} = \frac{\sum x}{n}$, we can substitute $y = 2x$ into the formula for the new mean $\bar{y}$:

$$\begin{aligned} \bar{y} &= \frac{\sum y}{n} \\ &= \frac{\sum 2x}{n} \\ &= 2 \left( \frac{\sum x}{n} \right) \\ &= 2\bar{x} \end{aligned}$$

Since the factor of $2$ can be factored out of the summation, the mean doubles. 

Original mean = $15$, so New Mean = $30$.

steps.title: Deduce the new standard deviation
steps.content:
The standard deviation formula is $\sigma_x = \sqrt{\frac{\sum (x - \bar{x})^2}{n}}$. For the new data $y=2x$, the mean is $\bar{y}=2\bar{x}$. Substituting these into the formula:

$$\begin{aligned} \sigma_y &= \sqrt{\frac{\sum (2x - 2\bar{x})^2}{n}} \\ &= \sqrt{\frac{\sum (2(x - \bar{x}))^2}{n}} \\ &= \sqrt{\frac{\sum 4(x - \bar{x})^2}{n}} \\ &= \sqrt{4} \times \sqrt{\frac{\sum (x - \bar{x})^2}{n}} \\ &= 2\sigma_x \end{aligned}$$

The standard deviation doubles. 

Original $\sigma \approx 3.69$, so New $\sigma \approx 7.38$.

steps.title: Deduce the new IQR
steps.content:
The Interquartile Range (IQR) is defined as $Q_3 - Q_1$. Since multiplying by a positive constant ($2$) preserves the order of the data, the quartiles correspond to the same positions in the dataset, just with doubled values.

New $Q_3 = 2Q_{3,original}$ and New $Q_1 = 2Q_{1,original}$.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{New IQR} &= 2Q_3 - 2Q_1 \\ &= 2(Q_3 - Q_1) \\ &= 2 \times \text{Original IQR} \end{aligned}$$

The IQR doubles. 

Original IQR = $8$, so New IQR = $16$.

steps.title: State final answers
steps.content:
Based on the algebraic deductions:

*   **Mean**: Doubles to $30$.
*   **Standard Deviation**: Doubles to $\approx 7.38$.
*   **IQR**: Doubles to $16$.

steps.title: Identify unequal class widths
steps.content:
Notice that the intervals (class widths) in the table are not all the same size. For example, the first interval is $10$ minutes wide, while the third interval is only $5$ minutes wide. 

Because the class widths are unequal, we cannot plot Frequency directly. Instead, we must calculate the **Frequency Density** for the vertical axis. This concept is part of **Topic 4: Statistics & Probability**.

The formula for frequency density is:

$$ \text{Frequency Density} = \frac{\text{Frequency}}{\text{Class Width}} $$

**Interval** $0 < t \leq 10$:

**Interval** $10 < t \leq 20$:
- Class Width $= 20 - 10 = 10$
- Frequency $= 18$

$$ \text{FD} = \frac{18}{10} = 1.8 $$

steps.title: Recall the mean formula
steps.content:
To find an estimate for the mean from grouped frequency data, we assume all data points in a class lie at the midpoint ($x_i$). The formula is:

$$ \bar{x} = \frac{\sum f_i x_i}{\sum f_i} $$

where $f_i$ is the frequency and $x_i$ is the midpoint of each class interval.

This concept is covered in **Topic 4: Statistics & Probability**, specifically under *Mean of grouped data*.

steps.title: Calculate class midpoints
steps.content:
First, we determine the midpoint for each time interval by averaging the lower and upper bounds.

- For $0 < t \leq 10$: $$\frac{0 + 10}{2} = 5$$
- For $10 < t \leq 20$: $$\frac{10 + 20}{2} = 15$$
- For $20 < t \leq 25$: $$\frac{20 + 25}{2} = 22.5$$
- For $25 < t \leq 35$: $$\frac{25 + 35}{2} = 30$$

steps.title: Calculate sum of products
steps.content:
Next, we multiply each frequency ($f_i$) by its corresponding midpoint ($x_i$) and sum the results to find $\sum f_i x_i$.

$$\begin{aligned} \sum f_i x_i &= (12 \times 5) + (18 \times 15) + (10 \times 22.5) + (10 \times 30) \\ &= 60 + 270 + 225 + 300 \\ &= 855 \end{aligned}$$

Finally, divide the sum by the total frequency ($\sum f = 50$) to find the estimate of the mean.

$$ \bar{x} = \frac{855}{50} = 17.1 \text{ minutes} $$

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Midpoint}_1 &= \frac{0+10}{2} = 5 \\ \text{Midpoint}_2 &= \frac{10+20}{2} = 15 \\ \text{Midpoint}_3 &= \frac{20+25}{2} = 22.5 \\ \text{Midpoint}_4 &= \frac{25+35}{2} = 30 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Identify the standard deviation
steps.content:
Since this is a sample of batteries (Sample A), we use the sample standard deviation, denoted as $S_x$ or $s_x$ on calculators (sometimes shown as $s_{n-1}$). Do not use $\sigma_x$, which is for populations.

From the calculator:
$$S_x \approx 50.494...$$

Rounding to 3 significant figures:

steps.title: Find mid-interval values
steps.content:
To work with grouped data, we first need to represent each interval by a single value. We use the **mid-interval value** (or midpoint) for each class, calculated as the average of the upper and lower boundaries.

$$ \text{Midpoint} = \frac{\text{Lower Bound} + \text{Upper Bound}}{2} $$

- $200 \le t < 240$: $\frac{200+240}{2} = 220$
- $240 \le t < 280$: $\frac{240+280}{2} = 260$
- $280 \le t < 320$: $\frac{280+320}{2} = 300$
- $320 \le t < 360$: $\frac{320+360}{2} = 340$
- $360 \le t < 400$: $\frac{360+400}{2} = 380$
- $400 \le t < 440$: $\frac{400+440}{2} = 420$

steps.title: Calculate the mean
steps.content:
The mean of grouped data is the sum of the products of frequencies and midpoints ($\,\sum fx\,$), divided by the total frequency ($\,\sum f = n\,$).

First, calculate $\sum fx$:
$$\begin{aligned} \sum fx &= 6(220) + 11(260) + 25(300) + 31(340) + 21(380) + 6(420) \\ &= 1320 + 2860 + 7500 + 10540 + 7980 + 2520 \\ &= 32\,720 \end{aligned}$$

Now divide by the sample size $n = 100$:
$$\bar{x} = \frac{32\,720}{100} = 327.2 \text{ h}$$

steps.title: Calculate sum of squared deviations
steps.content:
To find the standard deviation, we first determine the sum of the squared deviations from the mean for each group, weighted by frequency: $\sum f(x - \bar{x})^2$.

Using $\bar{x} = 327.2$:
$$\begin{aligned} \sum f(x - \bar{x})^2 &= 6(220 - 327.2)^2 \\ &+ 11(260 - 327.2)^2 \\ &+ 25(300 - 327.2)^2 \\ &+ 31(340 - 327.2)^2 \\ &+ 21(380 - 327.2)^2 \\ &+ 6(420 - 327.2)^2 \end{aligned}$$

Calculating these terms:
$$ = 68\,951.04 + 49\,674.24 + 18\,496 + 5\,079.04 + 58\,544.64 + 51\,671.04 $$
$$ = 252\,416 $$

steps.title: Calculate standard deviation
steps.content:
Since we are using a sample to **estimate** the population standard deviation, we use the unbiased estimator formula which divides by $n-1$ (instead of $n$).

$$ s_{n-1} = \sqrt{\frac{\sum f(x - \bar{x})^2}{n - 1}} $$

Substitute our values ($n=100$, so $n-1=99$):

$$\begin{aligned} s &= \sqrt{\frac{252\,416}{99}} \\ &= \sqrt{2549.656...} \\ &\approx 50.5 \text{ h} \end{aligned}$$

(Note: If you used $n=100$, you would get $50.2$, but for sample estimates, $n-1$ is the correct method.)

parts.markscheme:

6 marks total

steps.title: Construct cumulative frequency table
steps.content:
First, we construct the cumulative frequency table by adding the frequencies cumulatively for each interval.

*   $200 \le t < 240$: $6$
*   $240 \le t < 280$: $6 + 11 = 17$
*   $280 \le t < 320$: $17 + 25 = 42$
*   $320 \le t < 360$: $42 + 31 = 73$
*   $360 \le t < 400$: $73 + 21 = 94$
*   $400 \le t < 440$: $94 + 6 = 100$

The cumulative frequencies are: **6, 17, 42, 73, 94, 100**.

steps.title: Method for linear interpolation
steps.content:
To estimate the quartiles and median from grouped data, we use the linear interpolation formula:

$$ \text{Value} = L + \left( \frac{P - F_{before}}{f_{class}} \right) \times w $$

Where:
*   $L$ is the lower boundary of the class interval
*   $P$ is the position of the required statistic (e.g., $25^{th}$ value)
*   $F_{before}$ is the cumulative frequency of the previous interval
*   $f_{class}$ is the frequency of the current interval
*   $w$ is the class width

steps.title: Estimate the median
steps.content:
The median is the $50\%$ position. Since $N=100$, we look for the $50^{th}$ value.

Looking at the cumulative frequencies, the $50^{th}$ value falls in the interval $320 \le t < 360$ (since $42 < 50 \le 73$).

*   $L = 320$
*   $P = 50$
*   $F_{before} = 42$
*   $f_{class} = 31$
*   $w = 360 - 320 = 40$

Substituting these into the formula:

$$ \text{Median} = 320 + \left( \frac{50 - 42}{31} \right) \times 40 $$

$$ \text{Median} = 320 + \left( \frac{8}{31} \right) \times 40 \approx 330.32 $$

ti84.instructions: Calculate the interpolation expression

steps.title: Estimate the first quartile (Q1)
steps.content:
$Q_1$ is the $25\%$ position. We look for the $25^{th}$ value.

This falls in the interval $280 \le t < 320$ (since $17 < 25 \le 42$).

*   $L = 280$
*   $P = 25$
*   $F_{before} = 17$
*   $f_{class} = 25$
*   $w = 40$

$$ Q_1 = 280 + \left( \frac{25 - 17}{25} \right) \times 40 $$

$$ Q_1 = 280 + \left( \frac{8}{25} \right) \times 40 = 280 + 12.8 = 292.8 $$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the interpolation expression

steps.title: Estimate the third quartile (Q3)
steps.content:
$Q_3$ is the $75\%$ position. We look for the $75^{th}$ value.

This falls in the interval $360 \le t < 400$ (since $73 < 75 \le 94$).

*   $L = 360$
*   $P = 75$
*   $F_{before} = 73$
*   $f_{class} = 21$
*   $w = 40$

$$ Q_3 = 360 + \left( \frac{75 - 73}{21} \right) \times 40 $$

$$ Q_3 = 360 + \left( \frac{2}{21} \right) \times 40 \approx 363.81 $$

steps.title: Calculate the IQR
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the Interquartile Range (IQR) by subtracting $Q_1$ from $Q_3$.

$$ \text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1 $$

$$ \text{IQR} \approx 363.81 - 292.8 = 71.01 $$

Rounding to 3 significant figures:
*   Median $\approx 330$ h
*   $Q_1 \approx 293$ h
*   $Q_3 \approx 364$ h
*   IQR $\approx 71.0$ h

steps.title: Calculate the outlier step
steps.content:
The question asks us to use $$1.5 \times \text{IQR}$$ to find the outlier bounds. Let's calculate this value first:

$$1.5 \times 71.0 = 106.5$$

steps.title: Find the lower boundary
steps.content:
An outlier is any value smaller than the lower boundary. We calculate this by subtracting the step from the first quartile:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Lower Boundary} &= Q_1 - (1.5 \times \text{IQR}) \\ &= 292.8 - 106.5 \\ &= 186.3 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find the upper boundary
steps.content:
An outlier is also any value larger than the upper boundary. We calculate this by adding the step to the third quartile:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Upper Boundary} &= Q_3 + (1.5 \times \text{IQR}) \\ &= 363.8 + 106.5 \\ &= 470.3 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Compare with sample data
steps.content:
Finally, we check if any batteries in Sample A fall outside the interval $$[186.3, 470.3]$$. Looking at the frequency table:

- The minimum lifetime is $$200$$ h (start of the first interval).
- The maximum lifetime is less than $$440$$ h (end of the last interval).

Since $$200 > 186.3$$ and $$440 < 470.3$$, all data values are within the calculated boundaries.

steps.title: State the conclusion
steps.content: Since all values in the sample lie between the lower and upper boundaries, no outliers are expected.

parts.content: Give two reasons why Sample B should estimate the population mean more reliably than Sample A.
parts.markscheme:
- Award <marks>1</marks> for each valid reason. Max <marks>2</marks>.

Examples:
- Stratified sampling (by day) mitigates day/shift effects.
- Averages process variation across days; Sample A may be biased by one shift.
- Random selection each day reduces convenience/cluster bias found in Sample A.

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the sampling designs
steps.content:
First, let's identify the structure of each sample to understand their differences in reliability.

*   **Sample A** takes 100 batteries from a **single shift**. This is a form of cluster sampling where the cluster is one specific time period. It assumes that this one shift is perfectly representative of all production.
*   **Sample B** takes 10 batteries from **10 consecutive days**. This is a form of stratified sampling (stratified by time), where the sample is spread out over a longer period.

highlights.text: 10 batteries at random at the end of each of 10 consecutive days

steps.title: Reason 1: Reducing single-shift bias
steps.content:
The first reason Sample B is more reliable is that it mitigates anomalies associated with a specific time. 

Sample A is taken from a single shift. If there was a temporary issue during that specific shift—such as a machine calibration error, a different worker, or unusual temperature—the entire sample of 100 would be biased. Sample B minimizes this risk because an anomaly on one day would only affect 10% of the data, not 100%.

highlights.text: Sample A may be biased by one shift

steps.title: Reason 2: Capturing process variation
steps.content:
The second reason is that Sample B better captures the natural variation of the manufacturing process.

Manufacturing processes often fluctuate slightly from day to day. By spreading the sample over **10 consecutive days**, Sample B averages out these daily fluctuations, leading to an estimate that is more representative of the overall population mean compared to Sample A, which only sees the variation within a few hours.

steps.title: Identify the median position
steps.content:
To find the median from a frequency table, we first need to determine its position within the ordered data. The total frequency is $n = 60$. Using the formula for median position:

$$\text{Position} = \frac{n+1}{2} = \frac{60+1}{2} = 30.5$$

This indicates the median lies between the $30\text{th}$ and $31\text{st}$ data values.

gdc.instructions: Calculate (60+1)/2

steps.title: Calculate cumulative frequencies
steps.content:
We calculate the cumulative frequency (running total) for each category until we reach the position of the median ($30.5$).

- **0 pets:** Frequency is $15$. Cumulative frequency = $15$. (Positions $1$ to $15$)
- **1 pet:** Frequency is $12$. Cumulative frequency = $15 + 12 = 27$. (Positions $16$ to $27$)
- **2 pets:** Frequency is $10$. Cumulative frequency = $27 + 10 = 37$. (Positions $28$ to $37$)

steps.title: Locate the median value
steps.content:
We need the $30\text{th}$ and $31\text{st}$ values. The cumulative frequency for "1 pet" ends at $27$, and the cumulative frequency for "2 pets" reaches $37$.

This means that all data values from position $28$ to $37$ are equal to $2$. Therefore, both the $30\text{th}$ and $31\text{st}$ values are $2$.

$$\text{Median} = 2$$

steps.title: List data frequencies
steps.content:
To find the median using the **Eliminating Extremes** approach, we list the number of pets (values) and their frequencies. Our goal is to peel away layers from the outside (lowest and highest values) until we reach the center.

- **0 pets:** $15$
- **1 pet:** $12$
- **2 pets:** $10$
- **3 pets:** $8$
- **4 pets:** $10$
- **5 pets:** $5$

steps.title: Eliminate first layer
steps.content:
We compare the frequencies at the two extremes: **0 pets** ($15$) and **5 pets** ($5$).

We subtract the smaller frequency ($5$) from both ends:
- Remove $5$ from the top (exhausts all **5 pets**)
- Remove $5$ from the bottom ($15 - 5 = 10$ remaining for **0 pets**)

**Remaining:**
- **0 pets:** $10$ left
- **1 pet:** $12$
- **2 pets:** $10$
- **3 pets:** $8$
- **4 pets:** $10$

steps.title: Eliminate second layer
steps.content:
Now the new extremes are **0 pets** ($10$ left) and **4 pets** ($10$).

The counts are equal, so we eliminate $10$ from both:
- Remove $10$ from **4 pets** (exhausted)
- Remove $10$ from **0 pets** (exhausted)

**Remaining:**
- **1 pet:** $12$
- **2 pets:** $10$
- **3 pets:** $8$

steps.title: Eliminate third layer
steps.content:
The current extremes are **1 pet** ($12$) and **3 pets** ($8$).

We subtract the smaller frequency ($8$) from both:
- Remove $8$ from **3 pets** (exhausted)
- Remove $8$ from **1 pet** ($12 - 8 = 4$ remaining)

**Remaining:**
- **1 pet:** $4$ left
- **2 pets:** $10$

steps.title: Eliminate fourth layer
steps.content:
The extremes are now **1 pet** ($4$ left) and **2 pets** ($10$).

We subtract $4$ from both:
- Remove $4$ from **1 pet** (exhausted)
- Remove $4$ from **2 pets** ($10 - 4 = 6$ remaining)

Since only one category remains (**2 pets**) with a positive count, this is our median.

steps.title: State the median
steps.content:
The process has isolated the middle value.

Median $= 2$

steps.title: Identify the formula
steps.content:
To find the mean number of pets from a frequency table, we use the formula for the weighted mean:

$$ \bar{x} = \frac{\sum fx}{\sum f} $$

Where:
- $x$ is the number of pets
- $f$ is the frequency (number of people)
- $\sum fx$ is the sum of the products of each value and its frequency
- $\sum f$ is the total frequency (total number of people)

steps.title: Calculate sum of products
steps.content:
We calculate $\sum fx$ by multiplying each number of pets by its corresponding frequency and summing the results:

$$\begin{aligned} \sum fx &= (0 \times 15) + (1 \times 12) + (2 \times 10) + (3 \times 8) + (4 \times 10) + (5 \times 5) \\ &= 0 + 12 + 20 + 24 + 40 + 25 \\ &= 121 \end{aligned}$$

steps.content:

$$ \sum f = 15 + 12 + 10 + 8 + 10 + 5 = 60 $$

steps.title: Calculate the mean
steps.content:
Now we divide the sum of products by the total frequency:

$$ \text{Mean} = \frac{121}{60} $$

$$ \text{Mean} \approx 2.02 \text{ (to 3 s.f.)} $$

parts.content: What is the probability that two people, chosen at random, both owned $4$ pets? Give your answer as a fraction in its simplest form.
parts.markscheme:
<marks> M1 </marks>: For correct combinations or probability calculation (e.g., $\frac{10}{60} \times \frac{9}{59}$ or $\frac{\binom{10}{2}}{\binom{60}{2}}$).
   
<marks> A1 </marks>: Final answer $\frac{3}{118}$.

steps.title: Analyze the event
steps.content:
The question asks for the probability that two randomly selected people **both** own 4 pets. Since we are selecting two different people from the group, this is a dependent event (sampling without replacement). 

We will calculate this by finding the probability of the first person having 4 pets and multiplying it by the probability of the second person having 4 pets.

highlights.text: two people, chosen at random, both owned 4 pets

steps.title: Identify totals from table
steps.content:
First, we need the total number of people and the number of people who own exactly 4 pets from the frequency table.

*   **Total number of people**: The question states this is 60.
*   **Number of people with 4 pets**: Looking at the column for "4" pets, the frequency is 10.

steps.title: Probability for first person
steps.content:
The probability that the first person selected owns 4 pets is the number of people with 4 pets divided by the total number of people.

$$P(\text{1st person has 4 pets}) = \frac{10}{60}$$

steps.title: Probability for second person
steps.content:
After selecting the first person, we do not put them back into the group. This changes the totals for the second selection:

*   **Remaining people with 4 pets**: $10 - 1 = 9$
*   **Remaining total people**: $60 - 1 = 59$

The probability for the second person is:

$$P(\text{2nd person has 4 pets}) = \frac{9}{59}$$

steps.title: Multiply probabilities
steps.content:
To find the probability that **both** events happen, we multiply the individual probabilities:

$$P(\text{Both have 4 pets}) = \frac{10}{60} \times \frac{9}{59}$$

steps.title: Simplify final answer
steps.content:
Now we simplify the fraction:

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{10}{60} \times \frac{9}{59} &= \frac{1}{6} \times \frac{9}{59} \\ &= \frac{1 \times 9}{6 \times 59} \\ &= \frac{9}{354} \end{aligned}$$

Dividing the numerator and denominator by their greatest common divisor (3):

$$\frac{9 \div 3}{354 \div 3} = \frac{3}{118}$$

steps.title: Identify relevant frequencies
steps.content:
First, we identify the total number of people and the number of people who own exactly 4 pets from the table.

*   Total number of people: $60$
*   People with 4 pets: $10$

steps.title: Set up combinations formula
steps.content:
Using the combinations method, the probability is the number of ways to choose 2 people with 4 pets divided by the number of ways to choose any 2 people from the total group.

$$P(\text{both owned 4 pets}) = \frac{\binom{10}{2}}{\binom{60}{2}}$$ 

This notation $\binom{n}{r}$, also written as $^nC_r$, represents the number of ways to choose $r$ items from a set of $n$.

steps.title: Calculate favorable combinations
steps.content:
Calculate the number of ways to choose 2 people from the 10 who own 4 pets:

$$\binom{10}{2} = \frac{10 \times 9}{2 \times 1} = 45$$

gdc.instructions: Menu > Probability > Combinations > nCr(10, 2)

ti84.instructions: Press MATH, go to PRB, select nCr, then enter 10 nCr 2

steps.title: Calculate total combinations
steps.content:
Calculate the total number of ways to choose 2 people from the entire group of 60:

$$\binom{60}{2} = \frac{60 \times 59}{2 \times 1} = 1770$$

gdc.instructions: Menu > Probability > Combinations > nCr(60, 2)

ti84.instructions: Press MATH, go to PRB, select nCr, then enter 60 nCr 2

steps.title: Determine probability
steps.content:
Now, divide the number of favorable combinations by the total number of combinations:

$$P = \frac{45}{1770}$$

steps.title: Simplify the fraction
steps.content:
Finally, simplify the fraction by dividing the numerator and denominator by their greatest common divisor (15).

$$\frac{45 \div 15}{1770 \div 15} = \frac{3}{118}$$

steps.title: Recall the mean formula
steps.content:
To estimate the mean from grouped frequency data, we use the formula:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{\sum f_i x_i}{\sum f_i}$$

where:
- $f_i$ is the frequency of each interval
- $x_i$ is the midpoint of each interval
- $\sum f_i$ is the total number of parcels

steps.title: Calculate interval midpoints
steps.content:
First, we find the midpoint ($x_i$) for each weight class by averaging the lower and upper boundaries ($\frac{\text{lower} + \text{upper}}{2}$).

- For $0 < w \leq 2$: 

$$x_1 = \frac{0+2}{2} = 1$$

- For $2 < w \leq 4$: 

$$x_2 = \frac{2+4}{2} = 3$$

- For $4 < w \leq 5$: 

$$x_3 = \frac{4+5}{2} = 4.5$$

- For $5 < w \leq 8$: 

$$x_4 = \frac{5+8}{2} = 6.5$$

Now, calculate the frequency density for each class using the formula:

$$\text{Frequency Density} = \frac{\text{Frequency}}{\text{Class Width}}$$

*   **Class 1:** $\frac{10}{2} = 5$
*   **Class 2:** $\frac{20}{2} = 10$
*   **Class 3:** $\frac{15}{1} = 15$
*   **Class 4:** $\frac{30}{3} = 10$

These values will be the heights of the bars.

*   **1 goal:** Add next frequency $7$. Cumulative count: $4 + 7 = $ **11**. (Values 5th to 11th)

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify favorable frequencies
steps.content:
To find the probability of a match having $3$ or $4$ goals, we first need to identify the number of matches for each of these outcomes from the frequency table:

*   Matches with $3$ goals: **5**
*   Matches with $4$ goals: **3**

steps.title: Sum favorable outcomes
steps.content:
We sum these frequencies to find the total number of matches that satisfy the condition ($3$ or $4$ goals):

$$5 + 3 = 8$$

steps.title: Calculate probability
steps.content:
The probability of an event is the number of favorable outcomes divided by the total number of outcomes. The question states there are $30$ matches in total.

$$P(3 \cup 4) = \frac{\text{favorable matches}}{\text{total matches}} = \frac{8}{30}$$

steps.title: Simplify the fraction
steps.content:
Finally, we simplify the fraction by dividing the numerator and denominator by their greatest common divisor, which is $2$:

$$\frac{8}{30} = \frac{4}{15}$$

steps.title: Identify total number of matches
steps.content:
First, identify the total number of outcomes. The question states that the data comes from the last $30$ matches, which acts as our denominator for probability calculations.

$$\text{Total matches} = 30$$

steps.title: Find probability of 3 goals
steps.content:
From the frequency table, identify the number of matches where $3$ goals were scored. The frequency is $5$. 

The probability of selecting a match with exactly 3 goals is:
$$P(3 \text{ goals}) = \frac{5}{30}$$

steps.title: Find probability of 4 goals
steps.content:
Next, identify the frequency for $4$ goals from the table, which is $3$.

The probability of selecting a match with exactly 4 goals is:
$$P(4 \text{ goals}) = \frac{3}{30}$$

steps.title: Add the probabilities
steps.content:
Since a match cannot have both 3 goals and 4 goals at the same time, these events are **mutually exclusive**. To find the probability of "3 or 4 goals", we add the individual probabilities together.

Using the formula for mutually exclusive events ($P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)$):

$$\begin{aligned} P(3 \text{ or } 4) &= P(3) + P(4) \\ &= \frac{5}{30} + \frac{3}{30} \\ &= \frac{8}{30} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the calculation: 5/30 + 3/30

ti84.instructions: Type 5/30 + 3/30 and press ENTER. Use Math > Frac to convert to fraction.

steps.title: Simplify final answer
steps.content:
Finally, simplify the fraction by dividing the numerator and denominator by their greatest common divisor, which is 2.

$$\frac{8}{30} = \frac{4}{15}$$

steps.title: Identify complementary outcomes
steps.content:
To use the **Complementary Probability** approach, we first identify the outcomes that are *not* the ones we want. 

The question asks for the probability of scoring 3 or 4 goals. The complementary (opposite) event is scoring any *other* number of goals.

From the table, the other possible goal counts are **0, 1, 2, 5, and 6**.

steps.title: Sum complementary frequencies
steps.content:
Next, we find the total frequency for these complementary outcomes by adding their values from the table:

- 0 goals: 4
- 1 goal: 7
- 2 goals: 8
- 5 goals: 2
- 6 goals: 1

Sum of complementary frequencies:
$$4 + 7 + 8 + 2 + 1 = 22$$

steps.title: Calculate complement probability
steps.content:
The probability of the complementary event (not scoring 3 or 4 goals) is the sum of these frequencies divided by the total number of matches ($30$).

$$P(\text{not } 3 \text{ or } 4) = \frac{22}{30}$$

steps.title: Subtract from 1
steps.content:
Since the sum of probabilities for all possible outcomes is 1, we can find the probability of our target event (3 or 4 goals) by subtracting the complementary probability from 1.

$$P(3 \text{ or } 4) = 1 - P(\text{not } 3 \text{ or } 4)$$ 

$$P(3 \text{ or } 4) = 1 - \frac{22}{30}$$ 

$$P(3 \text{ or } 4) = \frac{30}{30} - \frac{22}{30} = \frac{8}{30}$$

parts.content: A match is chosen at random from the $30$ matches. Find the probability that this match had fewer than $6$ goals.
parts.markscheme:
- Identifying frequencies $4, 7, 8, 5, 3, 2$ (or their sum $29$) <marks>M1</marks>
- Probability is $\frac{29}{30}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify favorable outcomes
steps.content: The question asks for the probability of a match having **fewer than 6 goals**. This corresponds to the outcomes of $0$, $1$, $2$, $3$, $4$, and $5$ goals.

steps.title: Sum the frequencies
steps.content:
We sum the frequencies for all these specific outcomes using the table:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Sum} &= 4 + 7 + 8 + 5 + 3 + 2 \\ &= 29 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the calculation:

ti84.instructions: Type the sum of the frequencies into the home screen:

steps.title: Calculate the probability
steps.content:
The probability of an event is the number of favorable outcomes divided by the total number of outcomes. The question states there are $30$ matches in total.

$$P(\text{goals} < 6) = \frac{\text{Number of matches with } < 6 \text{ goals}}{\text{Total number of matches}}$$

$$P(\text{goals} < 6) = \frac{29}{30}$$

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content: The probability is $\frac{29}{30}$.

steps.title: Strategy: Complement Method
steps.content:
To find the probability of scoring **fewer than 6 goals**, we could add up the frequencies for 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5 goals. However, using the **Complement Method** is much faster.

The only outcome from the table that is *not* "fewer than 6 goals" is scoring exactly **6 goals**. 

We can find the probability of this single outcome and subtract it from 1.

steps.title: Find probability of the complement
steps.content:
First, identify the probability of the complement event: scoring 6 goals.

From the question, we know there are **30 matches** in total. Looking at the table, the frequency of scoring **6** goals is **1**.

$$P(6 \text{ goals}) = \frac{1}{30}$$

steps.title: Subtract from 1
steps.content:
Since the sum of all probabilities is always 1, we can subtract the probability of our complement from 1 to find the answer:

$$\begin{aligned} P(\text{fewer than } 6) &= 1 - P(6 \text{ goals}) \\ &= 1 - \frac{1}{30} \\ &= \frac{30}{30} - \frac{1}{30} \\ &= \frac{29}{30} \end{aligned}$$

steps.content:
The probability that the match had fewer than 6 goals is:

$$\frac{29}{30}$$

steps.title: Identify key frequencies
steps.content:
First, we identify the total number of matches and the specific number of matches where 2 goals were scored from the table.

*   Total matches: $30$
*   Matches with 2 goals: $8$

This information is found in the frequency table provided in the question.

steps.title: Apply Conditional Probability Rule
steps.content:
We need to find the probability of selecting two matches with 2 goals in a row **without replacement**. We use the multiplication rule for dependent events:

$$P(A \text{ and } B) = P(A) \times P(B|A)$$

1.  **First selection ($A$):** The probability of picking a match with 2 goals from the total is $\frac{8}{30}$.
2.  **Second selection ($B|A$):** Since we do not replace the first match, there is one less match with 2 goals ($8-1=7$) and one less match in total ($30-1=29$). The probability is $\frac{7}{29}$.

steps.title: Calculate the probability
steps.content:
Now we multiply the probabilities of the two dependent events together:

$$P(2 \text{ goals and } 2 \text{ goals}) = \frac{8}{30} \times \frac{7}{29}$$

Multiplying the numerators and denominators:

$$\frac{8 \times 7}{30 \times 29} = \frac{56}{870}$$

steps.title: Simplify to lowest terms
steps.content:
The question asks for the fraction in its lowest terms. Both $56$ and $870$ are even numbers, so we can divide the numerator and denominator by $2$:

$$\frac{56 \div 2}{870 \div 2} = \frac{28}{435}$$

Checking for other common factors, $28$ is divisible by $2, 4, 7, 14, 28$. None of these divide evenly into $435$ (which ends in 5). Thus, the fraction is in simplest form.

**Answer:** $\frac{28}{435}$

parts.content: Two matches are chosen at random, one after the other, from the $30$ matches without replacement. Expressing the answer as a fraction in its lowest terms, find the probability that one match had $1$ goal and the other had $3$ goals.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate probabilities for one specific order: $\frac{7}{30} \times \frac{5}{29}$ OR $\frac{5}{30} \times \frac{7}{29}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Consider both possible orders and add them: $\left(\frac{7}{30} \times \frac{5}{29}\right) + \left(\frac{5}{30} \times \frac{7}{29}\right)$ or $2 \times \left(\frac{7}{30} \times \frac{5}{29}\right)$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Probability is $\frac{70}{870} = \frac{7}{87}$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify the two cases
steps.content:
We need to find the probability of choosing two matches where one had 1 goal and the other had 3 goals. Since the matches are chosen **one after the other without replacement**, there are two distinct orders (mutually exclusive cases) we must consider:

1. First match has 1 goal, then second match has 3 goals.
2. First match has 3 goals, then second match has 1 goal.

This uses the **sum of products** rule: we calculate the probability of each path (product) and then add them together (sum).

steps.title: Retrieve frequencies from table
steps.content:
From the frequency table, we identify the number of matches for the specific goal counts we need:
- Number of matches with **1 goal** = $7$
- Number of matches with **3 goals** = $5$
- Total number of matches = $30$

steps.title: Calculate P(1 goal then 3 goals)
steps.content:
First, we calculate the probability of picking a match with 1 goal, followed by a match with 3 goals.

- Probability of first match having 1 goal: $\frac{7}{30}$
- After one match is removed, $29$ matches remain. The number of matches with 3 goals is still $5$.
- Probability of second match having 3 goals: $\frac{5}{29}$

Multiplying these gives the probability for this specific order:
$$ P(1 \text{ then } 3) = \frac{7}{30} \times \frac{5}{29} = \frac{35}{870} $$

steps.title: Calculate P(3 goals then 1 goal)
steps.content:
Now, we calculate the probability of the reverse order: picking a match with 3 goals first, then one with 1 goal.

- Probability of first match having 3 goals: $\frac{5}{30}$
- After one match is removed, $29$ matches remain. The number of matches with 1 goal is still $7$.
- Probability of second match having 1 goal: $\frac{7}{29}$

Multiplying these gives:
$$ P(3 \text{ then } 1) = \frac{5}{30} \times \frac{7}{29} = \frac{35}{870} $$

steps.title: Sum the probabilities
steps.content:
Since either case satisfies the condition, we sum the probabilities of the two mutually exclusive outcomes.

$$\begin{aligned} P(\text{1 and 3}) &= P(1 \text{ then } 3) + P(3 \text{ then } 1) \\ &= \frac{35}{870} + \frac{35}{870} \\ &= \frac{70}{870} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Simplify final answer
steps.content:
Finally, we simplify the fraction to its lowest terms by dividing the numerator and denominator by 10.

$$ \frac{70}{870} = \frac{7}{87} $$

This corresponds to the markscheme requirement: $\frac{70}{870} = \frac{7}{87}$.

steps.title: Define the probability method
steps.content:
Since the order in which the matches are chosen does not matter for the final set (we just need one of each type), we can use the combinations formula $$^nC_r$$ (also written as $\binom{n}{r}$) to solve this.

The probability is given by:
$$P(\text{1 goal and 3 goals}) = \frac{\text{Number of ways to choose the specific matches}}{\text{Total ways to choose any 2 matches}}$$ 

This method uses the Combinations formula found in **Topic 1.10** of the syllabus.

steps.title: Calculate total possible combinations
steps.content:
First, we find the total number of ways to select any 2 matches from the total 30 matches. We use the combination formula $$^nC_r$$ where $n=30$ and $r=2$:

$$^{30}C_2 = \frac{30!}{2!(30-2)!} = \frac{30 \times 29}{2} = 435$$

There are 435 total pairs of matches possible.

gdc.instructions: Go to Run-Matrix > OPTN > PROB > nCr. Enter 30C2.

ti84.instructions: Press MATH > PROB > nCr. Enter 30 nCr 2.

steps.title: Identify frequencies for specific goals
steps.content:
From the table, we identify the number of matches available for each goal count:
*   Matches with **1 goal**: Frequency is **7**
*   Matches with **3 goals**: Frequency is **5**

steps.title: Calculate favorable combinations
steps.content:
We need to choose exactly one match from the 7 available "1-goal" matches and exactly one match from the 5 available "3-goal" matches.

We multiply the combinations for each independent choice:
$$\text{Favorable ways} = \, ^7C_1 \times ^5C_1$$
$$\text{Favorable ways} = 7 \times 5 = 35$$

There are 35 ways to choose this specific pair.

gdc.instructions: Enter 7 * 5

ti84.instructions: Enter 7 * 5

steps.title: Calculate and simplify probability
steps.content:
Now we divide the favorable ways by the total ways:

$$P = \frac{35}{435}$$

To express this fraction in its lowest terms, we divide the numerator and denominator by their greatest common divisor, which is 5:

$$P = \frac{35 \div 5}{435 \div 5} = \frac{7}{87}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 35 divided by 435. Use the a b/c button to convert to fraction if needed.

ti84.instructions: Enter 35/435 > MATH > Frac > Enter

desmos.instructions: Type 35/435 and click the fraction icon to simplify.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The probability that one match had 1 goal and the other had 3 goals is:

$$\frac{7}{87}$$

parts.content: Calculate the mean distance of the students hiding.
parts.markscheme:
- Find the midpoints: $\{10,30,50,70,90\}$ and products $f \cdot x$: $\{100,450,750,350,450\}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate totals: $n=\sum f = 50$ and $\sum (f \cdot x) = 2100$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate mean: $\text{Mean} = \frac{2100}{50} = 42$ metres <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the formula
steps.content:
To find the mean of grouped data, we use the formula:

$$\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum f_i x_i}{\sum f_i}$$

Where:
- $f_i$ is the frequency of each interval
- $x_i$ is the midpoint of each interval
- $\sum$ represents the sum of the values

This concept is part of **Topic 4: Statistics & Probability**, specifically calculating the **Mean of grouped data**.

steps.title: Find the midpoints
steps.content:
For grouped data, we assume all data points in an interval are located at the midpoint ($x$). Calculate the midpoint for each distance interval by averaging the lower and upper bounds:

$$\begin{aligned} 0 \text{ to } 20 &\rightarrow \dfrac{0+20}{2} = 10 \\ 20 \text{ to } 40 &\rightarrow \dfrac{20+40}{2} = 30 \\ 40 \text{ to } 60 &\rightarrow \dfrac{40+60}{2} = 50 \\ 60 \text{ to } 80 &\rightarrow \dfrac{60+80}{2} = 70 \\ 80 \text{ to } 100 &\rightarrow \dfrac{80+100}{2} = 90 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Multiply frequency by midpoint
steps.content:
Multiply the frequency ($f$) of each interval by its midpoint ($x$) to find the total distance contribution for that group ($f \cdot x$):

$$\begin{aligned} 10 \times 10 &= 100 \\ 15 \times 30 &= 450 \\ 15 \times 50 &= 750 \\ 5 \times 70 &= 350 \\ 5 \times 90 &= 450 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the product of frequency and midpoint for each row.

ti84.instructions: Enter 10*10, 15*30, etc. to verify each product.

steps.title: Calculate the totals
steps.content:
Sum the frequencies ($\sum f$) and the products ($\sum f x$) to substitute into our formula.

Total Frequency:
$$\sum f = 10 + 15 + 15 + 5 + 5 = 50$$

Sum of Products:
$$\sum f x = 100 + 450 + 750 + 350 + 450 = 2100$$

gdc.instructions: Sum the products: 100+450+750+350+450

ti84.instructions: Sum the individual products calculated in the previous step.

steps.title: Calculate the mean
steps.content:
Divide the sum of the products by the total frequency to find the estimated mean distance.

$$\text{Mean} = \dfrac{2100}{50}$$ 

$$\text{Mean} = 42 \text{ metres}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 2100/50

ti84.instructions: Divide the sum of products by the total frequency.

parts.content: The seeker has to catch the same number of people from each class interval. If you get caught, you are out. The seeker caught $k$ people from each interval such that the mean distance of those who are still in the game is 40. Find $k$.
parts.markscheme:
- State the new frequencies $f_i - k$ and the mean equation:
$$ \frac{\sum (f_i - k) x_i}{\sum (f_i - k)} = 40 $$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Substitute values:
$$ \frac{2100 - 250k}{50 - 5k} = 40 $$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Cross multiply and expand:
$$ \begin{aligned} 2100 - 250k &= 40(50 - 5k) \\ 2100 - 250k &= 2000 - 200k \end{aligned} $$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Solve for $k$:
$$ 100 = 50k $$
$$ k = 2 $$ <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Draw the histogram for those who are still in the game after the seeker has caught $k$ people from each interval.
parts.markscheme:
![Histogram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/plots/1ff63701-0140-4f15-b2db-7b3f6e950ba6.png)

- Draw x-axis with intervals 0-20, 20-40, 40-60, 60-80, 80-100 <marks>A1</marks>

- Plot bars with correct heights:
  - 0-20: height = 8
  - 20-40: height = 13
  - 40-60: height = 13
  - 60-80: height = 3
  - 80-100: height = 3 <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the goal
steps.content:
This question asks us to draw a frequency histogram for the remaining students. From the previous parts of the question (or by comparing the initial total of 50 students to the remaining total of 40 shown in the markscheme), we know that $k = 2$ students were caught from each interval.

A histogram is used for continuous data, so we must draw bars that touch each other (no gaps) with the correct frequency heights.

steps.title: Calculate remaining frequencies
steps.content:
We calculate the new frequency for each interval by subtracting $k=2$ from the initial frequencies (which were 10, 15, 15, 5, and 5). 

$$\begin{aligned} \text{0-20 m:} && 10 - 2 &= 8 \\ \text{20-40 m:} && 15 - 2 &= 13 \\ \text{40-60 m:} && 15 - 2 &= 13 \\ \text{60-80 m:} && 5 - 2 &= 3 \\ \text{80-100 m:} && 5 - 2 &= 3 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Set up the axes
steps.content:
Draw the axes for your histogram:

*   **x-axis (Distance):** Mark the interval boundaries at 0, 20, 40, 60, 80, and 100.
*   **y-axis (Frequency):** Use a scale that goes up to at least 13 (the maximum frequency).

steps.title: Draw the bars
steps.content:
Plot the bars using the calculated heights. Remember that for a histogram representing continuous data, the bars must be **continuous** (touching each other).

*   **0–20:** Height 8
*   **20–40:** Height 13
*   **40–60:** Height 13
*   **60–80:** Height 3
*   **80–100:** Height 3

Ensure each bar has a width of 20 units on the x-axis.

steps.title: Awarding of marks
steps.content:
According to the markscheme, marks are awarded as follows:

*   **A1:** For correctly drawing the axes with intervals 0–20, 20–40, etc.
*   **A1:** For plotting the bars with the correct heights (8, 13, 13, 3, 3).

steps.title: Convert to cumulative percentage

The question states that $77.5\%$ of trials were "slower" than $\lambda$. In the context of time, slower means taking *more* time, so these values are greater than $\lambda$.

The cumulative frequency graph displays the percentage of data *less than or equal to* a value. Therefore, we need to find the percentage of trials that were faster than or equal to $\lambda$ by subtracting from $100\%$.

$$100\% - 77.5\% = 22.5\%$$

parts.content: Create a frequency table with 4 equal class intervals and the appropriate frequencies.
parts.markscheme:
- Correct class intervals ($0 < t \leq 40$, $40 < t \leq 80$, etc.) <marks>A1</marks>
- Correct frequencies summing to 40 <marks>A1</marks>

| Time Interval ($t$) | Frequency |
| :---: | :---: |
| $0 < t \leq 40$ | 1 |
| $40 < t \leq 80$ | 8 |
| $80 < t \leq 120$ | 24 |
| $120 < t \leq 160$ | 7 |

2 marks total

<note>Accept frequencies of (2, 7) for the first two rows if consistent with student's reading of the graph.
Total frequency must be 40.</note>

steps.title: Determine the class intervals
steps.content:
This question relates to **Topic 4: Statistics & Probability**, specifically **SL 4.2** on Cumulative Frequency graphs.

The question asks for a table with **4 equal class intervals**. First, we identify the total range of the time ($t$) from the x-axis of the graph, which goes from 0 to 160.

We calculate the width of each interval:

$$\text{Width} = \frac{\text{Maximum Time}}{\text{Number of Intervals}} = \frac{160}{4} = 40$$

The four intervals are:
- $0 < t \leq 40$
- $40 < t \leq 80$
- $80 < t \leq 120$
- $120 < t \leq 160$

steps.title: Read cumulative frequencies
steps.content:
Using the **Cumulative Frequency Differences** method, we first read the cumulative frequency ($CF$) value at the upper boundary of each interval directly from the graph curve.

- At $t = 40$: The curve is just above 0. Let's read this as $$CF(40) = 1$$.
- At $t = 80$: The curve is just below 10. Let's read this as $$CF(80) = 9$$.
- At $t = 120$: The curve is above 30. Let's read this as $$CF(120) = 33$$.
- At $t = 160$: The curve reaches the maximum. $$CF(160) = 40$$.

steps.title: Calculate interval frequencies
steps.content:
The frequency for each interval is found by subtracting the cumulative frequency of the previous interval from the current one.

**Interval $0 < t \leq 40$:**
$$\text{Frequency} = CF(40) - CF(0) = 1 - 0 = 1$$

**Interval $40 < t \leq 80$:**
$$\text{Frequency} = CF(80) - CF(40) = 9 - 1 = 8$$

**Interval $80 < t \leq 120$:**
$$\text{Frequency} = CF(120) - CF(80) = 33 - 9 = 24$$

**Interval $120 < t \leq 160$:**
$$\text{Frequency} = CF(160) - CF(120) = 40 - 33 = 7$$

9-1
33-9
40-33

33-9
40-33

steps.title: Construct the final table
steps.content:
Now we arrange these values into the frequency table. As a final check, verify that the frequencies sum to the total number of balls:

$$1 + 8 + 24 + 7 = 40$$

| Time Interval ($t$) | Frequency |
| :---: | :---: |
| $0 < t \leq 40$ | 1 |
| $40 < t \leq 80$ | 8 |
| $80 < t \leq 120$ | 24 |
| $120 < t \leq 160$ | 7 |

steps.title: Understand the term
steps.content: To find the **modal class**, we need to identify the class interval that has the highest frequency. The "mode" in statistics refers to the most frequent data point or group.

parts.content: Calculate an estimate of the mean time.
parts.markscheme:
<marks> M1 </marks>: For using midpoints for each class interval (e.g., $7.5, 22.5, 37.5, 52.5, 67.5, 82.5$).
   
<marks> A1 </marks>: For calculating $\sum (\text{frequency} \times \text{midpoint}) = 3390$.
  
<marks> A1 </marks>: For calculating the estimate of the mean, $\frac{3390}{100} = 33.9$.

steps.title: Identify the method
steps.content:
To estimate the mean of grouped data, we act as if all the data in each class interval is located at the exact center of that interval. This center value is called the **midpoint** ($x_i$).

The formula for the estimated mean is:

$$ \bar{x} = \frac{\sum f_i x_i}{\sum f_i} $$

where $f_i$ is the frequency and $x_i$ is the midpoint of each class.

steps.title: Calculate midpoints
steps.content:
Find the midpoint for each time interval by averaging the lower and upper bounds: $\frac{\text{lower} + \text{upper}}{2}$.

*   $0 < t \le 15$: $\frac{0+15}{2} = 7.5$
*   $15 < t \le 30$: $\frac{15+30}{2} = 22.5$
*   $30 < t \le 45$: $\frac{30+45}{2} = 37.5$
*   $45 < t \le 60$: $\frac{45+60}{2} = 52.5$
*   $60 < t \le 75$: $\frac{60+75}{2} = 67.5$
*   $75 < t \le 90$: $\frac{75+90}{2} = 82.5$

steps.title: Calculate products
steps.content:
Multiply each frequency ($f_i$) by its corresponding midpoint ($x_i$) to find the total contribution of each class.

*   $18 \times 7.5 = 135$
*   $25 \times 22.5 = 562.5$
*   $32 \times 37.5 = 1200$
*   $15 \times 52.5 = 787.5$
*   $8 \times 67.5 = 540$
*   $2 \times 82.5 = 165$

steps.title: Sum the values
steps.content:
Now, sum the products to find the total estimated time ($\sum f_i x_i$) and verify the total frequency ($\sum f_i$).

**Sum of products:**
$$135 + 562.5 + 1200 + 787.5 + 540 + 165 = 3390$$

**Total frequency:**
The question states there are $100$ students, which matches the sum of frequencies:
$$18 + 25 + 32 + 15 + 8 + 2 = 100$$

gdc.instructions: Type the sum into the main run-matrix or calculation screen

ti84.instructions: Type the sum into the main screen

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into a new line

steps.title: Calculate the mean
steps.content:
Finally, divide the sum of the products by the total frequency.

$$ \text{Mean} = \frac{3390}{100} $$

$$ \text{Mean} = 33.9 \text{ minutes} $$

ti84.instructions: Divide the sum by the total frequency

desmos.instructions: Divide the sum by the total frequency

$$\begin{aligned} \text{0 to 15} &\rightarrow 7.5 \\ \text{15 to 30} &\rightarrow 22.5 \\ \text{30 to 45} &\rightarrow 37.5 \\ \text{45 to 60} &\rightarrow 52.5 \\ \text{60 to 75} &\rightarrow 67.5 \\ \text{75 to 90} &\rightarrow 82.5 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: State the estimated mean
steps.content:
Read the value for the mean ($\bar{x}$) from the calculator screen. The calculator sums the products of midpoints and frequencies ($3390$) and divides by the total frequency ($100$).

$$\bar{x} = 33.9$$

parts.content: Work out the percentage of students who exercised for more than $1$ hour.
parts.markscheme:
<marks> M1 </marks>: For identifying the correct frequencies for more than $1$ hour (e.g., $8 + 2 = 10$).
    
<marks> A1 </marks>: Percentage is $10\%$.

steps.title: Identify the time condition
steps.content: The question asks for the percentage of students exercising for **more than 1 hour**. Since the table records time ($t$) in minutes, we need to find the number of students where $t > 60$.

steps.title: Select the relevant intervals
steps.content:
Looking at the table headers, identify the intervals that satisfy the condition $t > 60$. These are the last two columns:

*   $$60 < t \leq 75$$
*   $$75 < t \leq 90$$

steps.title: Sum the frequencies
steps.content:
Using the **Direct Summation** method, add the frequencies (number of students) for these specific intervals:

$$\text{Total students} = 8 + 2 = 10$$

steps.title: Calculate the percentage
steps.content:
Divide the number of students who exercised more than 1 hour by the total number of students ($100$) to find the percentage.

$$\text{Percentage} = \frac{10}{100} \times 100\% = 10\%$$

steps.title: Identify the complementary method
steps.content:
To find the number of students who exercised for **more than 1 hour** (60 minutes), we can use the complementary approach. 

We will calculate the number of students who exercised for **60 minutes or less** and subtract this from the total number of students.

steps.title: Sum frequencies for 60 minutes or less
steps.content:
Identify the time intervals that are less than or equal to 60 minutes ($t \leq 60$). These are the first four columns of the table.

Sum their frequencies:
$$18 + 25 + 32 + 15 = 90$$

steps.title: Subtract from the total
steps.content:
Subtract the sum from the total number of students to find how many exercised for more than 1 hour:

$$\text{Total} - (\text{Sum } \leq 60) = \text{More than 60}$$

$$100 - 90 = 10$$

steps.title: Calculate the percentage
steps.content:
Finally, express this number as a percentage of the total student body.

$$\frac{10}{100} \times 100\% = 10\%$$
